nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, warm, low-oxygen freshwater: rice paddies, swamps, marshes, ditches, muddy streams, and floodplains; burrows in soft mud during dry seasons. An obligate air-breather that regularly surfaces.

decorPreferences: tight-fitting, weighted lid — mandatory (extreme escape risk)
deep soft substrate (sand or fine mud) for burrowing
PVC pipes or large ceramic tubes as hides
driftwood and dense planting (Java fern, Vallisneria) for cover
dim lighting or floating plants to reduce stress
air gap between water surface and lid for obligate air-breathing

diet.notes: The Swamp Eel is an obligate carnivore that primarily accepts live or fresh-dead meaty foods, including earthworms, nightcrawlers, large bloodworms, shrimp, and pieces of raw fish or prawn. Feeder fish can be offered occasionally but are best used sparingly. Most specimens refuse dried or pelleted foods and will need to be weaned onto them gradually through slow substitution if dried foods are desired. Live earthworms are generally considered the safest and most reliable long-term staple. Feedings every two to three days are sufficient; overfeeding should be avoided, as uneaten food breaks down quickly and organic waste accumulates rapidly in the low-oxygen conditions this species prefers.

goodWith: very large, robust fish too big to be swallowed (in a species-only or large predator display)
other large synbranchids of equal size (with extreme caution and ample space)
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed (virtually all common aquarium species)
tank-mates in general — species-only tank strongly recommended

imageCandidates.notes: Standard FishBase species image of Monopterus albus showing full body lateral view.
Wikimedia Commons public domain image; verify current availability.

summary: The Swamp Eel (Monopterus albus) is a legless, scaleless air-breather from Asian rice paddies and swamps that reaches up to 1 m. All individuals are born female and transition to male at maturity. Listed as Injurious Wildlife under the US Lacey Act; import and interstate transport in the US are prohibited.
careNotes: The Swamp Eel is an eel-shaped synbranchid entirely lacking pectoral and pelvic fins. It is an obligate air-breather — the buccal cavity (the mouth and throat lining) is highly vascularised and acts as a respiratory surface, meaning the fish must reach the water’s surface regularly. Blocking access to air will kill it, so an air gap must always be maintained between the water surface and the lid. A tight-fitting, weighted lid is essential: this species can squeeze through any gap even a fraction of its body diameter and is widely regarded as one of the most determined escape artists in the freshwater hobby. A minimum tank of 300 L with a 150 cm footprint is recommended, with at least 10 cm of soft substrate for burrowing; 15–20 cm is preferable. Water quality must be maintained diligently despite the species’ natural tolerance for low-oxygen environments, as high ammonia and nitrite remain lethal in captivity. Water temperature should be kept between 22–30°C; the species is sensitive to cold and should not be exposed to temperatures below 15°C. Copper-based medications must not be used in any tank housing this species, as it is sensitive to copper. The Swamp Eel is widely considered aggressive and predatory, and will consume any tank-mate small enough to fit in its mouth. A species-only setup is strongly recommended. Diet should consist of live or fresh meaty foods, with live earthworms as the preferred staple; see diet notes for full feeding guidance. Monopterus albus is listed as Injurious Wildlife under the US Lacey Act; import and interstate transport within the United States are prohibited. It is established as an invasive species in Florida (since the 1990s) and Georgia, where escaped or released aquarium fish have established breeding populations capable of devastating native fish and invertebrate communities.
breedingNotes: Monopterus albus is a sequential protogynous hermaphrodite — all juveniles begin as functional females and transition to males upon reaching reproductive maturity (typically at around 30–40 cm body length). In the wild, spawning occurs in bubble nests built in mud burrows or vegetation; the male guards the nest and free-swimming fry until they are independent. Breeding in aquaria has very rarely been documented and is not considered achievable under standard home aquarium conditions. The sex-change biology makes sexing juveniles impossible. For captive breeding attempts, a very large, naturalistic setup with deep mud substrate, stable warm temperatures (26–28°C), and seasonal simulation (simulated dry season followed by rising water level) may stimulate reproductive behaviour, but reliable success has not been reported outside specialist facilities.

primary.name: FishBase — Monopterus albus
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed adult size (to 100 cm TL), temperature range (22–30°C), native distribution (South and Southeast Asia, East Asia), habitat (rice paddies, swamps, ditches), obligate air-breathing biology, taxonomy (Synbranchidae, Synbranchiformes), absence of paired fins, and protogynous sex change. Also confirmed invasive establishment in Hawaii, Florida, and Georgia.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Monopterus albus
additional.notes: Cross-checked husbandry requirements: tank size, obligate air-breathing, escape risk, dietary preferences (live earthworms as staple), water parameters, conspecific aggression, and predatory temperament. Confirmed copper sensitivity and extreme escape risk.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Swamp Eel / Monopterus albus
additional.notes: Supplementary trade source. Provided hobbyist-level care guidance, confirmed live-food feeding approach, lid security requirements, and general warnings about invasive status and legal restrictions in the US.

additional.name: USDA APHIS — Injurious Wildlife: Monopterus albus
additional.notes: Regulatory source confirming USDA-listing as Injurious Wildlife under the Lacey Act (18 U.S.C. § 42). Documents import prohibition and restriction on interstate transport; describes established invasive populations in Florida (Broward, Miami-Dade, Hillsborough counties) and Georgia.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Swamp Eel profile
additional.notes: UK-based trade source. Confirmed protogynous hermaphroditism, obligate air-breathing, escape-artist behaviour, live-food preference, and advanced care rating. Also noted legal concerns for keepers importing into regulated jurisdictions.

fish.breedingNotes: The Swamp Eel is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning all juveniles begin life as females and transition to male at maturity, typically around 30–40 cm body length. In the wild, spawning takes place in bubble nests constructed in mud burrows, with the male guarding the nest and fry. Captive breeding has not been documented under standard aquarium conditions and would likely require a very large naturalistic setup with seasonal simulation to have any prospect of success.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: USDA-listed Injurious Wildlife under the Lacey Act; import and interstate transport restricted. Invasive in Florida and Georgia via aquarium releases. Reaches 1m; air-breather; sequential protogynous hermaphrodite.
